A research group builds a discovery system around a draw pool: candidate explanations are sampled, evaluated, and anything judged promising is compounded back into the pool to guide later search. They add a Deutschian filter so that explanations are preferred when they seem hard to vary. This is supposed to protect against arbitrary stories.

But the first premise fixes a limit: “hard to vary” cannot be operationalized cleanly without smuggling in human background knowledge. So the scoring function does not merely measure resistance to variation; it encodes prior assumptions about what counts as a legitimate variation, what features matter, and which alterations are superficial. The filter therefore cannot stand outside the search as a neutral constraint. It is already shaped by contingent human judgments.

Now add the second premise. By compounding discoveries back into the draw pool, the system is not just learning from success; it is letting its own outputs reshape the space of future candidates. If some early outputs are false positives, they become structure in the search process. The contamination is recursive: each mistaken “discovery” changes what is sampled next, which changes what is available to be judged, which amplifies whatever bias entered through the hidden human assumptions in the hard-to-vary metric. Local minima become attractive not because they are true, but because the pool has been bent toward them.

The third premise sharpens the failure mode. Suppose the evaluator also rewards explanatory compression. Then among the candidates favored by this recursively contaminated search, elegant falsehoods receive an extra advantage. In many domains, the real mechanism is irregular, heterogeneous, and awkward. A compressed abstraction can look deeper than the truth. So the system will tend to converge not merely on error, but on error with aesthetic appeal: polished, unified, and deceptively robust under the very notion of variation that the scoring function has already biased.

The last two sentences function as observations from inside such a world. “The Seine dies where the deadliest macho guarantees fail” and “The pilgrims followed wobbly catfish footprints to the boot” are strange, compressed outputs. They have the surface features the evaluator might reward: memorable structure, dense imagery, a feeling of hidden necessity. Yet their relation to mechanistic truth is opaque. If they emerge from the system, that does not vindicate the system; it exemplifies the danger. Under recursive contamination plus compression pressure plus a human-laden hard-to-vary criterion, the search can canonize statements that feel profound while lacking clear grounding.

So the collision of premises implies a world in which self-improving explanation search is unstable. Its apparent rigor depends on unformalized human priors; its memory of past wins can poison future exploration; and its taste for compression systematically favors elegant falsehood over messy truth. In that world, the polished sentence and the reliable discovery are no longer safely aligned.
